This means that no one needs to undergo KYC or sign contracts beforehand; it's an open and transparent network. Trustlessness and permissionlessness are the most significant technical characteristics of blockchain's native business activities. However, this single technical characteristic is clearly insufficient. Another characteristic of blockchain is its transparency. It's difficult to imagine banks or other financial institutions with very high requirements for privacy and compliance putting their entire business process on the blockchain; they would face a huge problem: "data exposure." Neither banks nor other financial institutions, including those with very high privacy requirements like medical data, are likely to operate directly on a digitally native blockchain. However, to date, AI has indeed brought tremendous economic vitality. Especially after AI evolved from large-scale models to AI agents, discussions now focus on how the AI ​​intelligent agent economy can unleash more than ten times the commercial value in the future. However, the same problem arises: data transparency. With completely transparent data, the AI-powered intelligent agent economy has significant flaws, and resolving these flaws requires privacy-preserving computing technology. Looking back at the development of blockchain technology, since the launch of the Bitcoin network in 2009, nearly 16 years of development have proven its enormous commercial and economic value. However, blockchain technology, represented by Bitcoin, does possess the characteristic of openness and transparency. Therefore, in 2015, traditional banks and government regulatory bodies began to propose the concept of "consortium blockchains/permissioned blockchains." The emergence of consortium blockchains and permissioned blockchains stemmed from the fact that the open transparency of public blockchain data prevented many compliant businesses from operating on distributed ledgers. However, while the emergence of permissioned blockchain technology like consortium blockchains alleviated privacy concerns to some extent—only permitted nodes could join and share data within the permitted scope—it also had significant drawbacks. Over the past decade, with the introduction of the consortium blockchain concept, we have seen two very large consortium blockchain organizations: one is R3, a global network of banks, and the other is Hyperledger, led by IBM. Ultimately, it was discovered that after ten years, they hadn't incubated any applications with broad commercial prospects. This led to the view that consortium blockchains might not be blockchains at all. In the context of the time, this did hold some truth. However, with the emergence of tokenization and the tokenization of traditional financial assets, consortium blockchains are making a comeback. We can see that major global banks are already running their own internal permissioned blockchains. However, these internal permissioned blockchains may only have a single node, confirming the blockchain's operation only within the bank—called "private blockchains." So, why is it making a comeback? Because when a globally renowned bank provides its customers with blockchain tokenization services, such as deposit tokenization, it doesn't need to solve the trust issue. It doesn't need a third party as a blockchain node to endorse the trust, because you already trust the bank; you are already its customer. You are simply using tokenization within the bank's account system to complete a remittance from New York to Hong Kong—a cross-border remittance in 2 minutes. Without tokenizing deposits, completing such a remittance could take up to two days, hence the resurgence of private blockchains. However, private blockchains also present a problem: when customers of two different banks conduct cross-border remittances, you still need to traverse the broader network of a single private blockchain. Therefore, consortium blockchains are being discussed again. For example, SWIFT, in conjunction with nine major global banks, is exploring how to use blockchain technology and deposit tokenization tools to solve cross-bank and cross-border fund transfers. This is still under discussion, but we have already seen the resurgence of consortium blockchain technology. However, when it comes to cross-bank transactions, a significant question remains: how much internal bank data should you allow your partners to see? Now, a new technology is emerging: privacy-preserving computation, including zero-knowledge proofs and fully homomorphic encryption. These technologies allow computation while protecting privacy, and the results are completely consistent with the plaintext computation results. These new technologies have actually existed for a while. I remember when Ethereum Devcon was held in Shanghai in 2016, many speakers mentioned privacy computing technologies such as zero-knowledge proofs and formal verification. However, we haven't seen widespread application yet because their performance is insufficient. The cost, time, efficiency, and efficiency of commercial applications don't support them. But what I understand now is that fully homomorphic encryption chips may be launched in the second half of this year, achieving a performance of approximately 1000 transactions per second. This is clearly sufficient for some commercial application scenarios, as some scenarios don't require real-time computation results; you might be willing to wait 10 minutes, half an hour, or an hour. Under fully homomorphic encryption, the integration of blockchain tokens, AI tokens, and fully homomorphic encryption privacy computing technology is necessary to truly create a completely disruptive innovation in the business model of the intelligent agent economy. These three technologies must be combined to achieve disruptive innovation. Meanwhile, if we imagine that zero-knowledge proofs and fully homomorphic encryption algorithms are commercially viable in terms of efficiency, performance, and cost, then blockchain technology may experience another resurgence. At that time, there might not be a need for private or consortium blockchains; all data could be encrypted and stored on public blockchains, and privacy protection technologies would be sufficient to meet the highest levels of global compliance requirements. This is very likely to happen within the next three to five years. Yesterday, Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin also spoke on this stage about Ethereum's development path over the next five years, mentioning that Ethereum doesn't need to compete to become the fastest chain, but only to maintain its decentralization and security. Because the "blockchain trilemma" is decentralization, security, and performance, he will address decentralization and security, leaving performance issues to hardware acceleration, algorithm optimization, and L2 and L3 networks to solve according to different application scenarios. I will give an example to illustrate how these three technologies will create new business models. For example, a hospital's medical data is extremely valuable, but its privacy protection requirements are also extremely stringent. In the future token economics model, all commercial institutions will be "token factories." With sufficiently robust zero-knowledge proofs or fully homomorphic encryption privacy computing technology, any hospital can turn its medical data into tokens. Anyone can access this data to calculate desired feature results, but at the same time, they cannot access any personal privacy data. Only when these three technologies are combined can traditional business models be disrupted, forming the ultimate form of the intelligent agent economy. Even with only AI tokens and privacy computing technology, the business logic still holds true; a hospital can still innovate its business model, but the scope of its business will be difficult to expand globally. Digital products and services are essentially global services. Without blockchain support, anyone wanting to use hospital data would need to physically visit the hospital, negotiate, sign agreements, and pay through a bank—clearly not the operating model of a "token factory." What should a "Token Factory" look like? It should utilize the permissionless and trustless nature of blockchain technology to transform all the hospital's data into AI Tokens, making them available to users worldwide. Anyone with needs can access the data 24/7, just like we use the Bitcoin or Ethereum networks now, without signing agreements or undergoing KYC. Accessing data consumes Tokens, and consuming Tokens completes the payment to the hospital. This combination represents the ultimate goal of the intelligent agent economy. The same applies to individuals. If your past years of medical examination and healthcare data are encrypted and stored on the blockchain, you can use a permissionless and trustless business model to request services from global insurance companies: "My encrypted data is here. You can use actuarial models, within a homomorphically encrypted environment, to calculate my data and provide me with the most cost-effective, efficient, and personalized insurance plan." Therefore, with the synergy of these three technologies, the way financial services are conducted will be completely transformed. Insurance brokers or intermediaries will no longer be needed, and you will no longer belong to any single financial institution, yet you will simultaneously be a potential customer of all financial institutions. You can seek your "optimal solution" across the entire network in a trustless and permissionless manner. I also want to emphasize that there is a misconception that AI Tokens are the monetary unit of the AI ​​intelligent agent economy. This is not the case. AI Tokens are the "means of production" of the intelligent agent economy, not a monetary unit. From electricity, chip computing power, large models, algorithms to applications, this is the five-layer structure of Token economics proposed by NVIDIA's Jensen Huang, which actually describes the production process of intelligent agent products or services. However, when you purchase or enjoy products or services, you need to pay with currency, and this currency can only be digital currency. This is because AI Agents cannot use the currency used by humans; they require programmable, subdivisible, and real-time settlement currency. Imagine an AI agent calling an API provided by someone else. It can't operate through a banking system; real-time value settlement is necessary, and each call might only amount to a few cents. Traditional banking payment systems cannot support such small, frequent payments. Therefore, programmable digital currency is the lifeblood of the intelligent agent economy. Furthermore, the intelligent agent economy will generate new digital assets. In this system, both currency and assets need to be tokenized. Otherwise, they cannot be understood or used by machines. Current currencies like the US dollar, Hong Kong dollar, and RMB are not programmable; only after tokenization can they be recognized and used by machines. In summary, the AI ​​intelligent agent economy is a trustless, permissionless business model that will significantly reduce business costs. Society currently operates a massive and expensive system to build business trust and prevent and punish dishonest behavior. This requires the entire society to share the operating costs. However, in trustless and permissionless blockchain business networks, this cost is unnecessary. Under such a low-cost, high-efficiency emerging business model system, new asset classes will emerge. The Crypto world has "native digital assets" like Bitcoin and Ethereum, as well as "twin digital assets" formed after being blockchaind in the real world. Similarly, the AI ​​world will also have native and twin digital assets. AI twin digital assets can be summarized as tokenization; AI native digital assets will emerge in large numbers in the next few years, building entirely new business models and giving rise to a completely new financial service system. Currently, our well-functioning financial service system is designed for "people." But in the future, a new generation of financial service systems, and even entirely new capital market systems, will definitely emerge specifically for AI, machines, and AI native digital assets. That concludes my presentation today. Thank you!
